summary: West German filmmaker (1945–1982), a central figure of New German Cinema. Bisexual; died at 37 having made over 40 films. Signed the Oberhausen Manifesto ('Papa's cinema is dead'). Major films: Ali: Fear Eats the Soul (1974, homage to Douglas Sirk), The Marriage of Maria Braun (1979, first of the BRD Trilogy), The Bitter Tears of Petra von Kant (1972), and the 15-hour Berlin Alexanderplatz TV miniseries (1980). Frequent cinematographer: Michael Ballhaus.

works.name: Ali: Fear Eats the Soul (1974)
works.description: Elderly German cleaning woman Emmi (Brigitte Mira) falls in love with and marries young Moroccan guest worker Ali (El Hedi ben Salem). The couple face ostracism and prejudice. In an outdoor cafe scene, the staff stare at them in an alienating manner. Emmi's son kicks in her television set at a family gathering — one of several references to Douglas Sirk's All That Heaven Allows, which the film directly homages. Ali is told by Emmi he must stop getting couscous. The final shot shows Emmi at Ali's hospital bedside — he has a stomach ulcer. Fassbinder himself plays Eugen, Emmi's bitter son-in-law. At the opening, a character tells the old man she is dancing with: 'Think much, cry much.' The film was also a reference point for Todd Haynes's Far From Heaven and John Waters's Polyester.

clues.clue: Elderly cleaning woman Emmi marries young Moroccan guest worker Ali; family member kicks in the TV; references Douglas Sirk's All That Heaven Allows; final shot: Ali in hospital with stomach ulcer, Emmi at his side
examples: the title character in the hospital with a stomach ulcer.
In one scene, a cleaning woman Emmi has an affair with the Moroccan guest worker Ali.
Ali: Fear Eats the Soul was directed by this man, who also starred in the film as Emmi's bitter son-in-law Eugen.

clues.clue: Outdoor cafe scene: couple sit as alienated staff stare at them from a distance; Fassbinder plays bitter son-in-law Eugen
examples: This film features a scene in which a crying couple sits in an outdoor cafe littered with yellow tables and chairs as the staff stare at them in an alienating manner from several feet away.

clues.clue: John Waters's Polyester, Todd Haynes's Far From Heaven, and this film are all homages to Douglas Sirk's melodramas
examples: John Waters's Polyester, Todd Haynes's Far From Heaven, and Rainer Werner Fassbinder's Ali: Fear Eats the Soul are all homages to this director's films, especially those starring Rock Hudson.

works.name: The Bitter Tears of Petra von Kant (1972)
works.description: Set in a bedroom dominated by a large reproduction of Poussin's Midas and Bacchus. The fashion designer Petra lures Karin (a fashion model) and begins a lesbian relationship. The silent assistant Marlene packs her suitcase and leaves at the end. Marlene and Petra dance disconsolately to 'Smoke Gets In Your Eyes' by The Platters. The film inspired the fictional play 'Maloja Snake' in Oliver Assayas's film Clouds of Sils Maria. The American Soldier, another Fassbinder film, ends with two men writhing on the ground to his co-written song 'Too Much Tenderness.'

clues.clue: Set in a bedroom dominated by a reproduction of Poussin's Midas and Bacchus; Petra lures Karin as a fashion model; silent assistant Marlene packs suitcase and leaves at the end; they dance to 'Smoke Gets In Your Eyes'
examples: A large depiction of Poussin's Midas and Bacchus is prominently featured in a film by this director that follows the relationship between Karin and the title character.
The silent assistant Marlene packs up her suitcase and leaves at the end of this director's film The Bitter Tears of Petra Van Kant.
Marlene and Petra dance disconsolately to 'Smoke Gets In Your Eyes' in The Bitter Tears of Petra Von Kant.

clues.clue: Inspired the fictional lesbian play 'Maloja Snake' in Oliver Assayas's Clouds of Sils Maria
examples: The lesbian relationship in the fictional play Maloja Snake in Oliver Assayas' film Clouds of Sils Maria is inspired by a film by this director, which is mostly set in a bedroom dominated by a reproduction of Pousin's Midas and Bacchus.

works.name: The Marriage of Maria Braun (1979) and BRD Trilogy
works.description: Maria Braun (Hanna Schygulla) kills the African-American soldier Bill as he brawls with her husband Hermann; Hermann takes the blame and goes to prison. Maria becomes the mistress of industrialist Karl Oswald. The film ends with a gas explosion. It is the first film of Fassbinder's BRD (Federal Republic of Germany) Trilogy, together with Lola (1981) and Veronika Voss (1982). Lola adapts the same source material as The Blue Angel: building commissioner von Bohm is corrupted by the prostitute Lola. Phoenix (2014, Christian Petzold) was influenced by The Marriage of Maria Braun.

clues.clue: Maria kills African-American soldier Bill; husband Hermann serves time; Maria becomes mistress of industrialist Karl Oswald; ends with gas explosion; first of the BRD Trilogy (also Lola and Veronika Voss)
examples: The title character is impregnated by the African-American soldier Bill, but kills him as he brawls with her husband.
It is the first film of a trilogy together with the same director's Lola and Veronika Voss.

The Blue Angel inspired this director's film about the corruption of the building commissioner von Bohm by the title prostitute, Lola; that film is included with another movie by this man that ends with a gas explosion in his BRD Trilogy.

works.name: Berlin Alexanderplatz (1980)
works.description: A 15-hour TV miniseries (sometimes described as 13 parts and an epilogue) based on Alfred Doblin's novel. Begins in 1928 when Franz Biberkopf is released from Tegel prison after killing his wife — the first section is titled 'The Punishment Begins.' Franz loses his arm when his brutish frenemy Reinhold pushes him out of a getaway car. Barbara Sukowa plays Mieze, who is murdered. The hallucinatory epilogue includes: a rear-projected atom bomb explosion with Glenn Miller's 'In the Mood,' Franz hoisted on a crucifix, and two ostentatiously-dressed angels.

clues.clue: 15-hour TV miniseries based on Alfred Doblin's novel; Franz Biberkopf released from Tegel prison in 1928; first section titled 'The Punishment Begins'; Franz loses arm when Reinhold pushes him from a getaway car; Barbara Sukowa plays Mieze (murdered); epilogue features atom bomb + Glenn Miller + Franz on a crucifix
examples: A 'film in 13 parts and an epilogue' first seen as a TV series by this director begins in 1928 when Franz is released from jail after killing his wife.
A scene from this film includes a rear-projected image of an atom bomb exploding accompanied by Glenn Miller's 'In the Mood' while the protagonist is hoisted on a crucifix.

works.name: Career, Style, and New German Cinema
works.description: Fassbinder was bisexual and died at age 37 having already made over 40 films. He was a major figure of New German Cinema alongside Werner Herzog and Wim Wenders. He signed the Oberhausen Manifesto ('Papa's cinema is dead'), a founding document of New German Cinema. His melodramatic period began with The Merchant of Four Seasons (fruit peddler protagonist). His cinematographer was Michael Ballhaus. He was heavily influenced by Douglas Sirk's American melodramas. His final film was Querelle (1982), adapted from Jean Genet's novel. Tom Stoppard adapted Nabokov's Despair for Fassbinder's first English-language film (Despair, 1978). In The Third Generation, the phrase 'the world as will and idea' activates a terrorist cell secretly funded by an industrialist who profits from increased demand for security systems.

clues.clue: Bisexual German director; died at 37 having made 40+ films; signed Oberhausen Manifesto ('Papa's cinema is dead'); New German Cinema alongside Herzog and Wenders
examples: For 10 points, identify this bisexual German director who had already made 40 films by the time he died at age 37, including Berlin Alexanderplatz, and Ali: Fear Eats the Soul.
Fassbinder was a signatory of this declaration, associated with the phrase 'Papa's cinema is dead.'

clues.clue: Final film was Querelle (Jean Genet novel); Tom Stoppard adapted Nabokov's Despair for Fassbinder's first English-language film; melodramatic period began with The Merchant of Four Seasons
examples: Rainer Werner Fassbinder's final film was an adaptation of a novel by this director of Un Chant d'Amour about the sailor Querelle.
This author adapted Nabokov's Despair for Rainer Werner Fassbinder's first English film.

comprehensive_summary:
Rainer Werner Fassbinder (1945–1982) was a West German filmmaker and a central figure of New German Cinema alongside Werner Herzog and Wim Wenders. Bisexual and relentlessly prolific, he died at age 37 having made over 40 films. He signed the Oberhausen Manifesto ('Papa's cinema is dead'). His cinematographer was Michael Ballhaus. Deeply influenced by Douglas Sirk's American melodramas.

Ali: Fear Eats the Soul (1974) is a direct homage to Sirk's All That Heaven Allows: elderly German cleaning woman Emmi (Brigitte Mira) marries young Moroccan guest worker Ali (El Hedi ben Salem). A son kicks in her TV set; an outdoor cafe scene depicts the staff staring at the couple in alienating fashion. Fassbinder himself plays bitter son-in-law Eugen. The final shot shows Emmi at Ali's hospital bedside (stomach ulcer). The film also inspired Todd Haynes's Far From Heaven and John Waters's Polyester.

The Bitter Tears of Petra von Kant (1972): set in a bedroom dominated by a large reproduction of Poussin's Midas and Bacchus. Petra lures Karin to become a fashion model; lesbian relationship. Silent assistant Marlene packs her suitcase and leaves at the end; Marlene and Petra dance disconsolately to 'Smoke Gets In Your Eyes.' The film inspired the fictional play 'Maloja Snake' in Oliver Assayas's Clouds of Sils Maria.

The Marriage of Maria Braun (1979, BRD Trilogy part 1): Maria kills the African-American soldier Bill; husband Hermann takes the blame. Maria becomes industrialist Karl Oswald's mistress; ends with a gas explosion. The BRD Trilogy also includes Lola (1981, building commissioner von Bohm corrupted by prostitute Lola — same source as The Blue Angel) and Veronika Voss.

Berlin Alexanderplatz (1980): 15-hour TV miniseries (13 parts + epilogue) based on Alfred Doblin's novel. Franz Biberkopf released from Tegel prison in 1928 (first section: 'The Punishment Begins'); loses arm when Reinhold pushes him from a getaway car; Barbara Sukowa plays Mieze (murdered). Hallucinatory epilogue: rear-projected atom bomb + Glenn Miller's 'In the Mood' + Franz on a crucifix.

Fassbinder's final film was Querelle (1982, adapted from Jean Genet). Tom Stoppard adapted Nabokov's Despair for Fassbinder's first English-language film (Despair, 1978). The melodramatic period began with The Merchant of Four Seasons. The Third Generation features 'the world as will and idea' as a terrorist cell's activation phrase.
recursive_suggestions: Douglas Sirk — Hollywood melodrama director who heavily influenced Fassbinder; All That Heaven Allows directly inspired Ali: Fear Eats the Soul (Fine Arts)
Werner Herzog — fellow New German Cinema director; frequent comparisons in bonus questions (Fine Arts)
Berlin Alexanderplatz (Doblin novel) — source for Fassbinder's 15-hour miniseries (Literature)
